grading.extracted_answer: To reconstruct the sequence, perform a post-order traversal of the initial tree. For each node u in the initial tree, identify the contiguous segments of its initial children [ui, ui+1, ..., uj] that are mapped to a single child v in the final tree. For each such segment, perform (j - i) merge operations: first merge ui with ui+1 to create a new node, then merge that new node with ui+2, and continue this process until only one node (representing the target child v) remains. The operations must be recorded in the order they are encountered during the post-order traversal to ensure that all children of a node are merged before any operations involving the node itself are processed.

Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging
Family: coding
Entry type: coding
Grader type hint: binary_hidden_tests

  "grader_type": "binary_hidden_tests"
}

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Authoritative reference:
{
  "correct_answer": null,

Submitted final result text:
FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence, perform a post-order traversal of the initial tree. For each node u in the initial tree, identify the contiguous segments of its initial children [ui, ui+1, ..., uj] that are mapped to a single child v in the final tree. For each such segment, perform (j - i) merge operations: first merge ui with ui+1 to create a new node, then merge that new node with ui+2, and continue this process until only one node (representing the target child v) remains. The operations must be recorded in the order they are encountered during the post-order traversal to ensure that all children of a node are merged before any operations involving the node itself are processed. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that all necessary merge operations within the subtrees of a node are completed before the node's own children are merged, maintaining the structural integrity required by the tree transformation rules.
